Barriers to Housing Supply

Four Case Studies from Hong Kong

By Erin Chan (2019)

Choi Hung Estate. Media: Drawn by author

Choi Hung Estate. Media: Drawn by author

Hong Kong's Housing Supply Is Hitting the Bottleneck

Hong Kong, one of the world’s most expensive housing markets, has long been plagued by a persistent housing shortage.

By 2018, the shortfall of public and private flats had already accumulated to more than 70,000 due to a lack of available sites, as statistics from the Housing Authority show. 

Image captions

    To address the shortfall, the government-appointed Task Force on Land Supply last year (2018) shortlisted eight options to boost the city’s land supply, including developing brownfield sites — degraded farmland occupied by businesses such as recycling operations — reclaiming land and converting part of the Fanling golf course.

    Apart from the shortage of land, various other factors slowing down the pace of housing construction in the city have been identified in the following four case studies on Kai Tak Development, Yue Man Square, Wang Chau Housing Scheme and Village Type Development respectively.

    Image captions

      Case Study I: Kai Tak Development

      Fifty-three-year-old Cheung Fuk-cheung, who has lived in Kowloon City all his life, still vividly remembers the last day of operations at Kai Tak Airport in July 1998.

      “Thousands of people, including me, gathered on the airport terminal roof to watch planes take off and land,” Cheung said.

      It was a surreal experience to see all the aircraft disappear overnight from the once-bustling airfield, he said. 

      Apart from holding fond memories of the former airport, Cheung has wondered why the area remains a vast construction site 21 years after operations were moved to Chek Lap Kok, instead of becoming what the government envisioned as “a redevelopment project that provides a quality living environment.”

      Image captions

        The Current Appearance of the Former Kai Tak Airport

        A view of Kai Tak North from Kowloon Bay. Photo: Ceeseven/WikiCommons

        A view of Kai Tak North from Kowloon Bay. Photo: Ceeseven/WikiCommons

        A view of Kai Tak South from Kowloon Bay. Photo: Ceeseven/WikiCommons

        A view of Kai Tak South from Kowloon Bay. Photo: Ceeseven/WikiCommons

        A view of the construction sites in Kai Tak North in July 2017. Photo: Wikipedia user -Wpcpey/WikiCommons

        A view of the construction sites in Kai Tak North in July 2017. Photo: Wikipedia user -Wpcpey/WikiCommons

        Metro Park, one of the few fully-established facilities in Kai Tak. Photo: Sonj Ng/Flickr

        Metro Park, one of the few fully-established facilities in Kai Tak. Photo: Sonj Ng/Flickr

        A view of the Kai Tak Terminal Cruise, one of the few fully-established facilities in Kai Tak in 2014. Photo: Wing1990hk/Flickr

        A view of the Kai Tak Terminal Cruise, one of the few fully-established facilities in Kai Tak in 2014. Photo: Wing1990hk/Flickr

        Kai Tak is a key component of the government's ambitious plan to achieve its 10-year housing target of 460,000 units by 2025/26, a goal announced in 2015. This initiative aims to address housing demands and foster sustainable urban development in the region.

        However, Kowloon City District Councillor Yang Wing-kit expressed concern, stating that there is still a long way to go. He described the public housing supply at the city's largest prime site as "painfully slow."

        Local environmental concern group Green Sense estimated that, as of April 2018, Kai Tak's public-private housing supply ratio had reached 40:60. This figure falls short of the 60:40 split implemented by the government from 2014 to 2017.

        The public housing supply ratio encompasses all four public housing estates, including one resettlement estate and one Home Ownership Scheme (HOS) court, which collectively provide a total of 14,531 units.

        "Kai Tak should act as a catalyst for the redevelopment process in the surrounding areas of Kowloon City by providing sufficient public housing," said Tam. "However, we have not seen that so far."

        Meanwhile, Roy Tam Hoi-bong, president of the environmental concern group Green Sense, criticized the government for the delays in private housing supply at the former airport, particularly on the old runway.

        "The runway section is ready for housing development," Tam stated. "Yet the government has not been making effective use of the area."

        Source: Long Term Housing Strategy Annual Progress 2018 by the Transport and Housing Bureau

        What has stalled the housing development at Kai Tak?

        1. Prolonged zoning processes

        Yang believed that the slow pace of housing construction in Kai Tak was due to the prolonged zoning process since the airport's relocation in 1998.

        It was not until 2007 that the Kai Tak Outline Zoning Plan (Kai Tak OZP) was approved, after undergoing multiple revisions following several rounds of public and inter-departmental consultations.

        He stated that the frequent amendments to draft OZPs were the main reason for the prolonged approval of the Kai Tak OZP.

        Under the Town Planning Ordinance, anyone can propose amendments to draft OZPs. After considering these changes, the Town Planning Board will submit a revised plan to the Chief Executive-in-Council for approval within the statutory period of nine months. However, this period may be extended by an additional six months if necessary.

        1.1 Inconsistent projections of Kai Tak's population

        Yang highlighted that inconsistent projections of Kai Tak's population over the years, in particular, had delayed the approval of the Kai Tak OZP.

        "This, in turn, affects the planned housing stock in the area," he said.

        The Feasibility Study for South East Kowloon Development, commissioned by the government in 1998, predicted that Kai Tak would house 320,000 residents. However, this estimate was finalized at 105,000 residents in 2007.

        The finalised population estimate was further refined in 2017, with the government increasing the figure to 134,000 residents.

        He stated that public calls to review the residential parameters had contributed to fluctuations in the projected population, influenced by factors such as the economic downturn in the late 1990s, the outbreak of SARS in 2003, and backlash against harbor reclamation in 2004.

        Kai Tak's projected population over the years. Source: Study reports under the Kai Tak Planning Review.

        1.2 Discussions regarding the residential density of different areas within Kai Tak have been ongoing

        Kowloon City District Councillor Ho Hin-ming of the Liberal Party stated that the frequently revised residential density of Kai Tak over the years had also delayed the approval of the Kai Tak OZP.

        Ho highlighted that the three-stage public consultation under the Kai Tak Planning Review, which took place from 2004 to 2006 and involved extensive discussions on the subject, further delayed the passage of the OZP.

        "There has been long-standing antagonism between two groups, particularly those who support higher residential density and those who advocate for lower residential density in different parts of Kai Tak," said Ho.

        "Some believe the runway area, with its elongated water frontage, should be developed for low-density private housing. However, others oppose this idea, arguing that it would benefit only a small group of wealthy individuals."

        "There are also citizens who advocate for a balanced mix of residential density throughout the entire area."

        Tam shared the same view regarding the lengthy approval of the OZP, stating that the prolonged negotiations over residential density in Kai Tak in recent years had exacerbated the delay.

        In 2017, the government proposed to the Town Planning Board to relax the residential density on 16 sites in the area, 11 of which are located on the runway. The plot ratios for these runway sites would be increased from 3.4 - 5.9 to 5.5 -7.

        Tam's concern group was among those opposed to the government's proposal, as they argued it would block ventilation in the Kowloon Bay area and worsen the "wall effect."

        "Apart from the Town Planning Board's two public consultations on the amendments, our group also consulted the public using a bottom-up approach," said Tam. 

        "I believe that is what has slowed down the approval of the Kai Tak OZP."

        1.3 Discovery of Ancient Relics in Kai Tak

        Fellow district councillor Jimmy Lam Pok said the archaeological Lung Tsun Stone Bridge remnants unearthed at the To Kwa Wan Station construction site of the Shatin to Central railway project in 2008 had further stalled the Kai Tak OZP. 

        “It is not as simple as just adding a preservation corridor for the bridge remnants,” said Lam.

        “There are also other considerations involved, such as the relocation of the two waterfront driveways, the future underground shopping mall connected to Kowloon City, and the route of the railway project.” 

        Lam added that it would be more time-consuming when the government conducted inter-departmental consultations on the revisions to the OZP.

        Image captions

          What Steps Could Have Shortened the Zoning Process in Kai Tak?

          To speed up the approval of the Kai Tak OZP, Tam said the government should have revisited its immigration and population planning policies. This revision could save significant time in projecting population estimates.

          "It is the influx of migrant population that is adding pressure to the housing stock in Kai Tak, not the scarcity of land," he said.

          The number of mainland immigrants entering Hong Kong via one-way permits reached 235,900 from 2012 to 2016, representing 90% of the incremental growth of the city's overall population, according to Legislative Council data.

          He added that the government should have established a comprehensive framework to accurately determine the amount of land reserved for residential purposes from the outset, rather than spending excessive time revising different residential parameters. 

          "The government has to continually revise residential parameters, such as development density and plot ratio, to maximize the housing supply."

          Land zoned for residential use in Kai Tak accounts for approximately 13% (43 hectares) of the total land area (328 hectares). This residential zoning is primarily concentrated in medium-density neighborhoods, the runway, and the north and south aprons of the site, according to the Lands Department.

          Image captions

            Yang stated that the government should also consider making effective use of available sites awaiting zoning for temporary purposes.

            "Those sites could have been repurposed for short-term uses, such as temporary parks with outdoor fitness equipment, in addition to car parking," said Yang.

            "Utilizing land for temporary purposes could even help expedite Kai Tak's overall zoning process, but the government has provided few specifics," he added.

            Image captions

              2. The Slow Release of Sites for Tender

              Ho also believed that the housing delays in Kai Tak were related to the slow release of land for tender.

              As of May 2019, 80% (22 out of 30) of the residential land parcels had not been sold via tender. The eight unsold lots include four on the north apron and four on the runway.

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Source: Drawn by author in accordance to the land sale records by The Lands Department

              Ho stated that the government's concerns about housing speculation and the adaptability of the housing market were part of the reason for the slow release of land for tender.

              In an email response from the Development Bureau, the authority noted that factors hindering the release of sites for tender include thorough consideration of market conditions and time-consuming procedures, such as the preparation of land sale documents.

              Image captions

                Ho also expressed concern that if the sites sold by tender are developed into housing too quickly, they may not be compatible with the surrounding environment. 

                "Housing construction should be implemented in stages, based on the readiness of nearby transport infrastructure," he said.

                "But Kai Tak still lacks comprehensive transport services. In addition to insufficient buses for commuters, the critical Shatin to Central link railway project has yet to be completed," he added.

                The opening date of the railway project has been delayed since late 2018, primarily due to the suspension of construction work for archaeological studies of ancient relics at the project site, as well as repeated failures in safety tests in recent years. 

                Image captions

                  He added that if the residential parcels near the Kai Tak Sports Podium had been sold and developed too quickly, it could have impacted the construction of the infrastructure, which only began in the first quarter of this year.

                  Image captions

                    Yang also believed it was reasonable to release sites for tender based on their maturity in recent years.

                    "Some of these sites are designated for temporary uses through short-term tenancies, such as temporary car parks and construction facilities for the Shatin to Central Link MTR project or the Central Kowloon Route Highway project," said Yang. 

                    Yang suggested that the government should place all remaining sites in Kai Tak onto the "reserve list" of its upcoming Land Sales program at once, rather than releasing a specific number of parcels for tender each year. 

                    "Regardless of any future large-scale infrastructure projects that may emerge unexpectedly in Kai Tak and consequently affect the number of sites released for tender," he said.

                    "Placing all the sites on the reserve list from the beginning is a prudent move, as the government can then earmark the sites for tender immediately based on their readiness for development."

                    He noted that the suggested measure could also be applied to other areas of the city.

                    Image captions

                      What Should Be Done to Speed Up Housing Construction in Kai Tak?

                      Tam stated that a crucial step for the government to expedite public housing construction in Kai Tak is to reserve the remaining lots for public housing or Home Ownership Scheme (HOS) uses.

                      "From the construction of the foundation to the completion of a housing estate, it takes an average of two years," said Tam.

                      "Therefore, the time it takes to build public housing units depends on when the government will release the land and whether it will be designated for public housing purposes."

                      Ho suggested that the government commence all types of housing projects and their construction shortly after sites are allocated or successfully bid on by developers in land sales. He also recommended establishing a timeline for when the projects would be completed.

                      "For instance, the government's new proposal could mandate that housing projects and their construction be initiated within a week after the land has been allocated or sold to developers," said Ho.

                      "And all housing units would be completed within three years," he added.

                      In an effort to boost the city’s public housing supply, the government announced earlier this year that it would allocate seven private housing sites in Kai Tak, situated near two future MTR stations—Sung Wong Toi and Kai Tak—along the Shatin to Central Link, for public housing use.

                      Image captions

                        Tam believed that if Ho’s suggestion were applied to the government’s new measure, the time required to complete all the housing units could be shortened from an estimated seven years to three years. 

                        "The government's new measure is a step in the right direction for addressing the public housing crunch in Kai Tak and the city," said Tam.

                        "However, it will not address the issue in the short term unless their housing projects and construction work are initiated immediately," he added.
